The frequency doubled beam was polarized by a Glan–Thomson prism with a polarization ratio of 10−6. A zero-order λ/2-plate mounted on a stepping motor precisely rotated the orientation of the incoming field with respect to the layer.

We placed a mount in front of the objective lens that allowed us to insert various beam shaping elements, such as beam stops of various size. The objective lens was a NA = 1.4 oil immersion lens (Leica 100× , Planapo, Wetzlar, Germany). It is connected with a semiaperture angle of 67.3°, which is the highest for immersion lenses. Annuli were produced by obstructing the central part of the beam with a black circular foil attached to a plane parallel glass plate, as indicated in Figs 1 and 4. The diameters of the circular obstructions were 3.7, 4.5 and 5.0 mm, so that they amounted to 66, 80 and 89% of the diameter of the aperture, respectively. The obstruction was aligned in the beam by observing the transmitted excitation light as a guide. Figure 4 reveals that we also employed a cross-like
